does: This paper addresses machine unlearning in multi-task settings where models share backbone parameters across multiple tasks. It introduces two unlearning scenarios: full-task unlearning that removes a target instance from all tasks, and partial-task unlearning that removes supervision from selected tasks only. The core contribution is identifying and formalizing task-level and instance-level interference—where forgetting data from one task inadvertently degrades performance on other tasks or instances due to shared parameters. The paper proposes an interference-aware framework combining task-aware gradient projection, which constrains weight updates to task-specific subspaces, with instance-level gradient orthogonalization that reduces conflicts between forget and retain signal directions. Experiments on multi-task computer vision benchmarks demonstrate the approach effectively removes target data while preserving performance across non-target tasks and instances.
connects: This work extends prior machine unlearning research, which has primarily tackled single-task forgetting scenarios, to the more realistic multi-task paradigm that dominates modern deep learning. It builds on existing unlearning methods that typically use gradient-based approaches to selectively remove training data influence, but identifies a critical limitation: shared backbone architectures create parameter coupling that causes unintended side effects. The paper connects to multi-task learning literature on parameter sharing and task interference, adapting insights about task-specific subspaces and gradient projection methods from continual learning and multi-task optimization. It also relates to recent work on machine unlearning in federated and distributed settings where task heterogeneity poses similar challenges to maintaining fine-grained forgetting guarantees.
enables: This framework opens avenues for practical unlearning in multi-task and multi-user deployment scenarios common in real-world systems like foundation models adapted across multiple downstream tasks. It enables more granular privacy-preserving methods where different stakeholders can request forgetting for different subsets of tasks without compromising other users' model performance. The interference-aware approach could extend to continual learning settings where new tasks are added sequentially, allowing selective forgetting of old tasks while protecting newer ones. Future work might generalize the method to deeper task hierarchies, apply it to language models and other domains, or develop theoretical guarantees on unlearning completeness under multi-task interference. The gradient orthogonalization technique could also inspire improvements in other multi-objective optimization problems where conflicting gradients must be managed.
